claims.claim: We investigate whether optimizing the position of retrieved evidence chunks within a fixed-token prompt budget can improve answer quality in citation-style QA, beyond what standard top-k similarity packing achieves.

claims.claim: We implement an adaptive "searched position lottery" packer that selects candidate-pool search under tight token budgets and falls back to kept-set-preserving reordering at higher budgets, using a lost-in-middle position objective without access to ground-truth labels.

claims.claim: This ordering places the most similar evidence first, but a growing body of work on the "lost in the middle" phenomenon suggests that language models attend disproportionately to content at the beginning and end of long contexts, with degraded utilization of middle-positioned evidence.

claims.claim: If position matters, then the default similarity-ranked ordering may be suboptimal: the most relevant evidence chunk might be more effective if placed at a position the model attends to more strongly, even if that means a less-similar chunk occupies the lead position.
